comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is upon us and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This is my eighty-second and most recent published book, a book on UFOs somewhat unoriginally titled The UFO Book. It's from the same publisher as my titles The Occult Book and The Conspiracy Book, and like them it's a coffee table book, with 100 vignettes from the history of unexplained things in the sky. It's not like my other book on the subject, The UFO Chronicles, in that I don't propose explanations -- this is purely a chronology of remarkable sightings from around the world and across the years. What's behind them? That's for you to decide. It was a fun project, and should be fun to read. Interested? You can get copies here if you live in the US, and at your favorite online or bricks-and-mortar bookstore otherwise. 

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Peace Pilgrim
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I was curious as to whether you'd ever heard of a woman who went by the name Peace Pilgrim. Having read her book, she seems to represent an amazing example of a wandering mystic in the modern age. You can see an informational website about her at https://www.peacepilgrim.org/.

From 1953 until her death in a car accident in 1981, she walked across the United States covering over 25,000 miles on foot. She had no possessions but her clothes and a few personal care items, having vowed to fast until given food and sleep in the open unless given shelter. She preached a message of nonviolence to anyone who would listen, giving sermons in churches and making several TV appearances over the years.

Her book given an account of her spiritual development that closely follows patterns seen in other mystics and saints. Given that she was active during most of the Cold War, I wonder if her influence helped prevent it from going hot. Whether or not you've heard of her I think her story could be interesting for your readership. Are there any similar figures from the 20th or 21st centuries you can think of?

comments.title: Assisted dying
comments.content: What does the western esoteric/occult tradition(s) say about the ethics and impact of what is called medically-assisted dying (i.e. euthanasia)? Would there be any distinction between this and ordinary suicide? I have a friend who has been suffering tremendously for 15 years due to severe iatrogenic harm, and has been approved for assisted dying in Europe. She finds solace in knowing that is available (she was approved for it 2 or 3 years ago, so all she would have to do is "say the word" and it could be arranged within weeks), but a large part of her naturally does  not want to go that route. In my view there is real healing possible for her and we have been exploring those possibilities, but she faces a tough, demanding road back to health, regardless. My thinking is that- while "assisted dying" may end the pain on the physical plane for now, the spiritual and karmic layers she is working through would remain to be dealt with on other planes and in future incarnations. What do you think? I guess what I am asking is, is the idea of assisted dying as an "end to suffering" valid in some sense, or is it all illusory? Even if the karmic work remains to be done,  is there any benefit to a person/soul to getting a reset and facing it in another lifetime, possibly with some wisdom and experience they are unable to access now in a body wracked with pain and difficulty? Appreciate any perspective you have!

replies.title: Re: Assisted dying
replies.content: It's completely illusory. Death is a transition, not an end, and if you choose to die to try to run away from suffering, it'll be waiting for you between lives and in your next incarnation. It's precisely by dealing with the pains and difficulties we face that we can clear negative karma and free ourselves to go on to other things.

replies.title: Re: Assisted dying
replies.content:
not the OP:

From a purely occult perspective, what are the impacts of "pulling the plug" on a patient who is in a comatose state?

replies.title: Re: Assisted dying
replies.content: That depends on whether the person would survive without machinery. If they're being kept alive by machines, turning the machines off is an act of mercy that allows nature to take its course. If they're alive and breathing without mechanical help, killing them is murder.

1) Look at your birth chart, and find a date and time when the planets ruling the signs on the cusps of your 2nd and 10th houses are well dignified. If possible, get a waxing moon applying by trine or sextile to one of them.

1a) It you have a garden, grow vervain in it -- that brings prosperity. Any protective herb placed in or around your workplace will chase off bad luck and bring good fortune. Oh, and mistletoe has that among its many other powers.

You may find that people very quickly get used to the fact that you're the local cunning man (as male white witches were called in England and America back in the day), and start inquiring about more overtly magical things.

replies.title: Re: The business of teaching a simple SoP
replies.content:
1.) thank you!

2.) there is none. Out here in the hinterlands the only expressions that people know are “die Hexe” and “der Zauberer”, but both are void of any supernormal meaning. They both have equivalents over the border in Slavic lands, and equally devoid of supernormal connotations.
Things get interesting once you start talking about “Brauch”, the root of “Braucher” (practitioner), it means something like “the old ways of doing things” or “the old use”. That would be “stare šege” in a Slavic dialect. And this has some significance, people still give some respect to it.
The people who get a reputation are usually “Heilpraktiker” but those usually work with given systems like spagyrics, magnetism, astrology, or have a spiritual retreat. Or are questionable mediums. This is the land of Paracelsus after all.
Once you get west, to the borderlands to Italy there are “shamen”, or “vrač” in slovenian, and there people start whispering nervously.

replies.title: Contemplating the draft
replies.content:
I was born in 1959, and was aware of the War in Vietnam years before I could have been drafted. My strategy was to focus on math, engineering, and communications, in an effort to make myself too valuable behind the lines to be allowed near The Front. The draft was abandoned before I had to register, and I was too old to register when it was reinstated. But I can't complain about the highly-motivated self-education I got from poring over the ARRL Radio Amateur's Handbook, and Mark's Mechanical Engineering Handbook, and the engineering career that followed.

replies.title: the draft
replies.content:
Back in the late 1960s I knew a Swiss man who had done his Swiss army service then moved to Canada and was drafted there. He was in Calif. when I knew him and said the only thing that kept him out of the US army was that he had lost part of one finger while in Canada and was 4f as a result.

People I know who have been in the military do not favor a draft. I have been told it is difficult enough to turn volunteers into disciplined and trained troops. The thought of having to train and discipline scores of people who don't want to be there just horrifies them.

Another reason that the draft is unlikely to be revived short of another Pearl Harbor type event is that the question of drafting women would inevitably arise. The level of discord that would arise would be huge. I have always been comfortable saying that women should not be drafted because I also opposed drafting men, but with current laws and attitudes about the roles of women in society anyone proposing a draft would have to face the question.

Rita

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I was in college when the event JMG describes, the reinstatement of the US draft registration requirement, took place (in the political wake of the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan). I listened carefully to several of the groups who were instructing and encouraging students on how to claim conscientious objector status, and from that, realized I wasn't one. At that point, I decided that if drafted I'd take my chances at being a warrior and just do my best. Which surprised me. When I was a kid in the 60s I'd been terrified of being drafted someday, and my parents and relatives were anti-military (not surprising given what their parents had emigrated from Germany to escape) and had told me they'd have moved to Canada to keep me from being drafted if it had become necessary.

I'm not saying that's what anyone else should do, just that it's one option for facing the possibility.

comments.title: Is Big Tech Cursed?
comments.content:
I've been thinking a lot about the intersection of magic and the internet lately, and have started to wonder if part of the problem is that the tech world is under the influence of a self-inflicted curse. As I understand it, curses are caused by recognizing (on some level) that a pattern of thinking and behavior held will lead to some sort of doom, and then refusing to do anything about changing the problematic pattern of thinking and action, and that the curse works out as a consequence of the frantic effort to do anything other than the one thing that will prevent the horrific fate.

Well, if this is the case, then it seems like there is a good case to be made that there is a self imposed curse on the tech world. Quite a few people correctly anticipated exactly why an advertising business model would inevitably lead to disaster, including, most famously, Google's founders Larry Page and Sergey Brin.

The problem though is that there is so much money to be had with advertising, especially with the promise of targeting individuals, and some of the core myths of the internet make it very hard for people to imagine alternatives, even as they are thriving. The result is that nearly all of these people who correctly explained why advertising would corrupt and ruin the internet eventually dove into an advertising business model; and their prior concerns were proven right. This seems like a phenomenal way to induce a self-imposed curse, and would explain a lot of the oddities of the tech world.

Do you think this sort of analysis is plausible?

replies.title: Re: Is Big Tech Cursed?
replies.content:
That makes enormous sense.

"You who Google holds in thrall,
Share its feast and share its fall!"

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content:
Excellent! Yes, it's good to add the SoP, but the other two legs of the magical cauldron -- daily meditation and daily divination -- are very well provided for with the Coelbren, and the methods of practical magic will be a very useful addition.

In general, one of the best and quickest ways to become a competent occultist is to take some one system or set of symbols and work them thoroughly, exploring all the ways they can be put to work. It's not a path often taken in our superficial age, but it works.

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content: Some letters aren't things you should use in magic. Chi is a great example -- if you happened to be an evil magician, you could use it to make people quarrel, but I don't recommend it for karmic reasons! Lli, similarly, is probably not something you'd want to use in magic as it causes turbulence and trouble. On the other hand, some difficult letters have good practical uses -- O, Di or Ti, for example, could be used in a "cut and clear" working to let go of a connection to someone or something that you want to release from your life. Meditate on each of the letters and explore their uses, and you'll figure all this out.

replies.content: As far as I know, you and your friends are the first to try to work through the system as a group since the 1920s, if it even happened then. (I have no records from that era.) Thus I have no advice to offer, just my encouragement and blessing.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch
replies.content:
I'm still working through it, in the first grade of the Greater Mysteries, but one potential challenge I might anticipate for a group from how it's gone for me (and I seem to remember hearing from others as well) is that the grades rely on self-judgement for completion. Depending on one's temperament, that might lead to rushing through things that deserve some more time and practice, or stalling out on one or more practices trying to get it "perfect" when it's actually plenty good for moving on. In a group, you might get lucky and have these tendencies balanced out, or if everyone is alike, they could be exacerbated, or if y'all each go at your own pace, you might end up at wildly different places over the same amount of time.

For what it's worth, whether it's the material or the kind of folks it attracts, or both, it also seems to lean more toward "taking longer than strictly necessary" than "prone to rushing through," so I'd especially look out for that.

So, it might be prudent to discuss a bit how y'all will handle judging when it's time to move on to the next grade (deadlines? majority vote? consensus?).

Cheers,
Jeff

I just finished reading The Secret of the Temple earlier today. What a beautifully understated book. You tie together a large number of what appear to be disparate historical threads into a theory that is not only coherent but surprisingly plausible- not what I'm used to when reading about the Knights Templar or the Holy Grail.

And yet the implications should your theory prove correct are just huge to consider. Ancient peoples may have used this technological suite in a certain temple-specific way; future adopters could use different parts of it in different ways. I don't know that you would consider it your most important legacy, but very few people could have pulled together the facts that you did, and even fewer could have done so in such an even-handed style and with access to the kind of audience you do.

I've poked around in several of Francis Young's books on exorcisms and on Baltic Pagan religions before those countries were Christianized, and they're extremely good. He seems to me to be a fine scholar with an excellent command of the available primary sources and the languages (Latin, Lithuanian, Anglo-Saxon) in which they were written.

The Baltic nations were among the very last to be Christianized in Europe, Lithuania as late as 1387 CE. Pagan survivals persisted in the Baltic lands throughout the 20th century, and probably today as well. (The father of a former student of mine was a member of an old Baltic (Estonian) noble family with ancestral land that included a small lake. When she went with him to visit the place in the late 20th century, she was surprised to hear him describe annual rituals his family had done when he was a boy to acknowledge the non-Christian Goddess of their ancestral lake.)

To the best of my knowledge Young is an English Roman Catholic, which in England seems to entail a certain willingness to go one's own way apart from the national mainstream.

comments.title: Tamanous, grave dirt
comments.content:
A little while back, I finally found what I am certain is my tamanous. I had been making a consistent effort to do so for a while but a combination of Jungian practice, numerology, and a whole host of synchronicities lead me to her. She was someone that I knew before her unfortunate death some years ago. Once it became clear and I performed a ritual to establish the relationship, my luck really turned around. I received a new job that was perfect for me. Various longstanding difficulties have been resolving, and the guidance she has provided for my individuation process has borne great fruit. There's been a lot of hard work involved but I couldn't have made all these changes on my own.

Recently, I felt a strong urge to visit her grave. I brought a silver coin and placed it in the topsoil of her grave, then took some of the soil. Most of it is in a container on the altar that I built to venerate her. But I also put a small amount into a pendant that I now wear around my neck. I feel a protective presence emanating from it.

Is any of this odd? I know that contact with the dead can be risky but I have been doing the Judson exercise and making regular offerings. She was such a saintly person in life, and I get the sense she's now a divine being. That's the feeling that her spiritual presence gives me.

replies.title: Re: Tamanous, grave dirt
replies.content: Any hoodoo doctor worth his or her salt would have advised you to do exactly what you did, right down to the silver coin as offering. "Divine" is a complicated word; in societies that are thoroughly polytheistic, it's quite common for the honored dead to end up in the lower ranks of divinity -- in Japan, for example, quite a few minor kami were human beings back in the day, and one I can think of was a cat...

replies.title: Re: Tamanous, grave dirt
replies.content:
I even drove home from the cemetery using a different route. Being about 45 minutes from my home, there were several different options for an alternate route. I'm curious about a few things, though.

1) Do we know what connection that grave dirt provides to the deceased? I'm assuming it has to do with the grave itself, and the more or less universal human practice of visiting the resting places of the deceased.

2) I'm not very familiar with Japanese practices. I've actually used some ideas from Chinese traditional religion to guide my religious devotion to my tamanous. Burning joss money and items, along with incense. But the Shinto kami you mention remind me of various Chinese deities who started out as a kind of ghost who rewarded living humans who made offerings. Do you know if there's much overlap between Shinto and traditional Chinese folk religion?

3) Can you recommend a good introduction to Hoodoo? Most of the practices I use now are rooted in Hoodoo but I've never tried any kind of comprehensive study and training system. With the Mayan Order lessons, I've already got a lot on my plate but a deeper study of Hoodoo seems to be next on my list.

replies.title: Re: Tamanous, grave dirt
replies.content:
1) Yes, but it also has to do with the dissolution of the etheric body of the dead person, which leaves an etheric trace in the soil.

2) That's kind of a hot-button question -- some scholars say yes, others say no. That said, polytheist traditions from all over have this same custom, and the Christian concept of sainthood isn't ultimately all that different.

3) There are plenty of books but all the ones I know of focus on specific techniques and traditions. Cat Yronwode's hoodoo correspondence course is very good, but I don't know if it's still available -- if it is, you'll find it at Lucky Mojo Curio Co.

replies.title: Re: Tamanous, grave dirt
replies.content:
1) Does that etheric trace disappear over time? That is, does the soil need to be replaced from time to time on my altar and in the pendant?

2) I had sainthood, in particular that of folk saints, in mind and almost asked a question along those line. Catholicism, especially, seems to be implicitly polytheistic in practice.

3) This course? https://www.luckymojo.com/mojocourse.html

replies.title: Re: Tamanous, grave dirt
replies.content:
1) No. What you have is a relic, and those retain power over millennial time scales.

2) Polytheism in monotheist drag, yes.

3) That's the one. I completed it and have my certificate of graduation.

replies.title: Re: Tamanous, grave dirt
replies.content:
2) LOL, that is well put.

As always, thanks for the answers!

replies.title: Re: Tamanous, grave dirt
replies.content:
in older times, before coffins began to be interred in underground cement vaults (a 20th century custom), the earth on old graves would sink downward as the coffins disintegrated and collapsed. Once that had happened, the dirt above the dead could and would absorb effluvia from the dead body. Graveyard dirt from such a grave, taken from right above the collapsed coffin, was thought to be especially potent for magic, as it actually contained physical traces of the dead. One can't hardly get that sort of graveyard dirt anymore.

I once saw such a recently collapsed 1800s grave in a cemetery about a mile from our house. It was very soon filled in by the cemetery staff so that there was no visible hint of its recent collapse.

replies.title: Re: Tamanous, grave dirt
replies.content: The power of first class relics is well documented in the history of Catholicism, so I am not surprised that this kind of "premodern" graveyard dirt is especially potent. I wonder, though, does having a lock, or even just a strand, of a deceased person's hair have some potency as well? I do not have any such relic from my tamanous, but do have locks of hair from deceased ancestors.

So many of us just don't think things through. Thanks for the training I get on Magic Monday. I wonder about connections I simply would not wonder about otherwise.

OtterGirl

comments.content: Do you have any recommendations for semi-permanent effective material for making astrological talismans? I'm trying to use something less permanent than stone and metal. I have tried wax. Is there such thing as de-consecrating a talisman when no longer needed? (if using stone/metal)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I use watercolor paper (available at any art supply store) that has been painted with a tea made of herbs corresponding to the planet that will be invoked. Let the paper dry completely before drawing and coloring the talisman. It will hold a charge very strongly, and once you're done with it, it can be deconsecrated and then burnt.

Yes, deconsecrating a talisman is very much a thing. In an outdoor place, light some incense charcoal, thank the energies you invoked for their help, then put a little asafoetida over the charcoal and cense the talisman with that. (This is done outside because burnt asafoetida stinks like nothing you've ever smelled.) Then rinse the talisman with cold water, lots of it.

comments.title: Etheric Plane
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Yesterday I had an experience while meditating in a group, where I felt my mind merged with the whole group, as well as my mind blending with objects around me.

Today, I find myself recovering energetically. But my perception is that the etheric plane around where I live seems to be quite over-extended or spread thin.

Is it possible that the ethers could react somehow to a mental event, by dispersing I throughout the atmosphere?

I appreciate your response…

replies.title: Re: Etheric Plane
replies.content: Every event on the higher planes tends to echo down the planes, all the way down to the material plane. In all probability, though, what's going on is that your etheric body is overstrained and needs rest and recuperation.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've been meditating on mapping the medieval hierarchy of powers (elementative, vegetative, sensitive and imaginative) onto the different planes (material, etheric, astral [none of them appear to deal with the mental plane to me]). It's simple enough to say that things that only exist on the material plane, like rocks, are only subject to the elementative powers. Vegetative powers (movement and reproduction, for the most part) are basically the beginning of life and therefore map to the etheric plane pretty well. Imaginative powers fit pretty cleanly onto the astral plane.

Where does sense perception fit? I believe the medieval understanding was that plants did not have senses and that this power came into play with animals. To some extent that makes sense to me; I think most plants do have a kind of sense perception but, as far as I know, don't have sense organs as such the way most animals do. The best I have come up with is to assign sensitive powers to the lower astral and imaginative powers to the higher.

I know that different categorization schemes rarely match up perfectly, but do you think there's anything important that I'm failing to account for, that might change this mapping? How would you match them?

Thank you for your time!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sense perception varies. Plants do have sense perception -- they perceive sunlight and rise toward it, and many of them have a sense of touch that allows them to twine around supports -- but their perception is much more diffuse than that of animals. If you think of plants having general senses and animals having specific senses, you won't be too far off. The rest of it sounds correct to me.

comments.title: Free will
comments.content:
JMG,

I'm sure you must have written about the topic of free will before, but I don't know where to find it.

What is your view on the matter? How much independence do I truly have in determining my "immediate" (this lifetime's) fate? How much latitude does my "overself" have?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Free will
replies.content:
Human beings are not born with free will -- they're born with the capacity to achieve free will, but must earn it, and even so it's never absolute. There are always things we can't control. With effort and discipline, though, we can develop "wiggle room" and then expand it, so that we gain some control over ourselves and our immediate surroundings. .

The Higher Self can do nothing until it is united with the lower self. The lower self descends into matter to learn freedom, and brings that gift back with it to the Higher Self.

comments.title: Propos of Nothing (again.)
comments.content:
So about three hours from me is a little town called Galena. Galena is the home of Ulysses S Grant, and, if it hadn't been for the Civil War and the fact that railroads supplanted rivers as the means of industrial transportation, Galena would have been the nexus of shipping in the US. Ah, well. These days its a tourist town, very quaint, with decent skiing and golfing, and a couple of really good places o eat.
But, for years. no occult bookstores. My wife always likes visiting the furniture or clothing stores. I always find myself twiddling my thumbs a lot.
But this year, lo and behold, there were at least three new places on the main street that sold tarot cards and the like. I'm interested in getting a Le Normand deck, and, well, no soap there. But one of these places did have quite a stack of Gypsy Witch decks. (Ah, at last, I hear at least one person thinking, I think we may be seeing a POINT to this ramble. Thanks for your patience).
So, when I was very young, maybe 14 or so, I had a Gypsy Witch deck, and, when JMG mentioned that the deck gifted to Justin Martense in Chorazin was based on the Gypsy Witch, I decided to look up a copy. And there it was. So that's it. No other point.
Oh, other than that this deck apparently is an entirely US creation, and has been in continuous production since its first appearance in 1903. Looking forward to looking into it again, after fifty-ish years.

replies.title: Re: Propos of Nothing (again.)
replies.content:
One of the reasons I used it as the deck that Sallie Eagle used for divination, and gave to Justin Martense, is precisely that it's an American divination deck, part of our own occult tradition. I modeled all the traditions of the Great Old Ones on classic American occultism, for that matter.

It's a fine deck. One of these days I may just do a tentacular version of the deck and see who's interested in it... ;-)

replies.title: Re: Propos of Nothing (again.)
replies.content: When I read the little I could find about the history of this deck I immediately saw that that was likely the reason. As I said, I'm looking forward to digging in to this a bit.
